title: Questioning an Unsafe Provider Order: The Duty to Clarify and the Chain of Command
subtitle: A med-surg nurse receives an order for digoxin 2.5 mg IV — about ten times the usual dose — for a 79-year-old in new atrial fibrillation. The nurse must hold the dose, clarify with the prescriber, and use the chain of command rather than carry out an unsafe order.

patient.name: M. Foster
patient.admitDate: Med-surg unit, new atrial fibrillation with rapid rate. A new order reads 'digoxin 2.5 mg IV now' — about ten times the usual dose. Nurse must clarify before acting.

vitals.temp: Do NOT give; call the prescriber

labs.v: Don't carry out an unsafe order.

labs.r: If prescriber insists and it's still unsafe.
Nurse is liable for unsafe care.

nursesNote: M. Foster, 79, is on the med-surg unit in new atrial fibrillation with a rapid rate. A new order reads 'digoxin 2.5 mg IV now' — roughly ten times the usual dose (about 0.125-0.25 mg), a classic tenfold/decimal error. The nurse has a legal and ethical duty NOT to carry out an order believed to be unsafe: the dose is held and not administered. The nurse rechecks the order, the usual safe dose range, and the patient (allergies, weight, renal function, current rhythm), then contacts the prescriber directly to clarify dose, route, and frequency. If the prescriber insists and the nurse still believes the order is unsafe, the nurse does not give it and escalates up the chain of command (charge nurse, nursing supervisor, pharmacy, and the medical chain). The nurse never alters the order independently and never silently omits it. Once a corrected, safe order is confirmed, it is carried out, and the clarification and resolution are documented objectively (who was contacted, when, what was discussed, and the outcome).
providerOrders: Hold the questioned dose; do not administer until clarified
Recheck the order against the usual safe dose and the patient's data
Clarify dose/route/frequency directly with the prescriber
If still unsafe, withhold and escalate up the chain of command
Document the clarification and resolution objectively

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does an unsafe order require?

opts.text: A dose about ten times the usual amount is a red flag to stop and clarify
The nurse has a duty not to carry out an order believed to be unsafe
The questioned dose should be held until it is clarified
Carrying out an order does not relieve the nurse of accountability for harm
If the provider wrote it, the nurse must give it without question
Illegible or ambiguous orders should also be clarified before acting
The nurse should contact the prescriber directly to clarify dose, route, and frequency
If the prescriber insists and it is still unsafe, the chain of command is used
The nurse should not change the dose independently to what seems right
Verbal or phone orders are read back and verified
Silently skipping the dose without telling anyone is acceptable
The clarification and outcome should be documented objectively

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the actions on recognizing the unsafe order

opts.text: Hold the dose; do not administer it
Recheck the order, the usual dose, and the patient's data
Contact the prescriber directly to clarify
If still unsafe, escalate up the chain of command
Give the corrected dose if clarified safe, and document
Hold the medication until the order is clarified
Compare the dose with the usual safe range
Call the prescriber to clarify a tenfold-looking dose
Use the chain of command if concerns aren't resolved
Document the clarification objectively
Administer the dose because the provider ordered it
Change the dose to what you think is right without contacting anyone
Silently skip the dose without telling anyone
Give it and just watch closely for toxicity

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Notify the prescriber of the concern and request clarification
Use read-back to confirm any new verbal order
If the prescriber insists and it's still unsafe, withhold it and call the charge nurse/supervisor
Involve pharmacy and the medical chain of command as needed
Document the clarification and resolution objectively
The nurse holds the dose and calls the prescriber to clarify
The nurse checks the usual dose range and the patient's data first
When the prescriber insists, the nurse escalates and still withholds the unsafe dose
The clarification and outcome are documented objectively
The nurse gives the tenfold dose because 'the doctor ordered it'
The nurse rewrites the order to a different dose independently
The nurse skips the dose silently without notifying anyone
The nurse administers the dose and plans to monitor for toxicity
